response:
PARAGRAPH 1
Developing robust code requires careful planning and precise logic to ensure accurate results when processing historical data sets. The task involves filtering a collection of date strings based on a specific century parameter provided by the user. Each date string follows the standard format of year month day separated by hyphens. To determine if a date belongs to a particular century, we must first extract the year component from the string. Once the year is isolated, we can calculate the corresponding century using simple mathematical operations. The formula for finding the century of any given year is straightforward yet crucial for correct implementation. Specifically, the century number equals the integer division of the year minus one by one hundred plus one. This calculation ensures that years from one to one hundred belong to the first century, while years from one thousand one to two thousand belong to the twenty first century. For instance, the year one eight hundred and one falls into the nineteenth century, whereas the year one nine hundred and one belongs to the twentieth century. This distinction is vital because the transition between centuries occurs at the start of a new hundred year period, not at the end of a round number. Many people mistakenly believe that the year two thousand marks the start of the twenty first century, but strictly speaking, the twenty first century began on January first two thousand and one. Our function must adhere to this strict definition to provide historically accurate filtering results. The implementation will iterate through every date string in the input list, parsing the year value and comparing it against the calculated range for the target century. We will use list comprehension for efficiency, as it is generally faster than traditional loops in Python for simple filtering tasks. This approach minimizes overhead and allows the function to handle large datasets with thousands of entries without significant performance degradation. Error handling is also important; we should ensure that invalid date formats or non-integer inputs do not crash the program. If a string does not match the expected format, it should be skipped gracefully to maintain data integrity. The final output will be a new list containing only the valid date strings that match the specified century criteria. This solution provides a clean and reusable tool for researchers analyzing historical timelines or demographic trends across different eras. By encapsulating the logic within a single function, we promote code modularity and ease of maintenance for future updates. The algorithm runs in linear time relative to the number of dates, making it optimal for large scale data processing needs. No complex data structures are required, keeping memory usage low and execution speed high. This efficiency is paramount when dealing with extensive archives
